Lasting; durable; long-suffering; as, an enduring disposition. "A better and enduring substance." Heb. x. 34.  -- En*dur"ing*ly, adv. T. Arnold.  -- En*dur"ing*ness, n.
In an equal manner or degree in equal shares or proportion; with equal and impartial justice; without difference; alike; evenly; justly; as, equally taxed, furnished, etc.
The time of evening; evening. [Poetic.] Spenser.
In an eventual manner; finally; ultimately.

In an express manner; in direct terms; with distinct purpose; particularly; as, a book written expressly for the young. The word of the Lord came expressly unto Ezekiel. Ezek. i. 3. I am sent expressly to your lordship. Shak.

A failing short; a becoming deficient; failure; deficiency; imperfection; weakness; lapse; fault; infirmity; as, a mental failing. And ever in her mind she cas about For that unnoticed failing in herself. Tennyson.
One who abstains from food.
First in time or place; most advanced; chief in rank or dignity; as, the foremost troops of an army. THat struck the foremost man of all this world. Shak.
In time past, either in time immediately preceding or at any indefinite distance; of old; heretofore.
The act of fulfilling; accomplishment; completion; as, the fulfillment of prophecy.
An artisan who manufactures vessels and ornaments, etc., of gold.
An Anglo-Indian name for a person just arrived from Europe. H. Kingsley.

The process of growing; the gradual increase of an animal or a vegetable body; the development from a seed, germ, or root, to full size or maturity; increase in size, number, frequency, strength, etc.; augmentation; advancement; production; prevalence or influence; as, the growth of trade; the growt
In a grudging manner.
Properly, a short hauberk, but often used loosely for the hauberk. Chaucer.
Who. [Obs.] In some Chaucer MSS.
The back of a horse.

Of or pertaining to a hypocrite, or to hypocrisy; as, a hypocriticalperson; a hypocritical look; a hypocritical action. Hypocritical professions of friendship and of pacific intentions were not spared. Macaulay. -- Hyp`o*crit"ic*al*ly, adv.
The quality or condition of being imperfect; want of perfection; incompleteness; deficiency; fault or blemish. Sent to my account With all my imperfections on my head. Shak.

To give, cause, or produce by striking, or as if by striking; to apply forcibly; to lay or impose; to send; to cause to bear, feel, or suffer; as, to inflict blows; to inflict a wound with a dagger; to inflict severe pain by ingratitude; to inflict punishment on an offender; to inflict the penalty o
Innocence.
of Kneel.
The state or quality of being lofty.

A public declaration, usually of a prince, sovereign, or other person claiming large powers, showing his intentions, or proclaiming his opinions and motives in reference to some act done or contemplated by him; as, a manifesto declaring the purpose of a prince to begin war, and explaining his motive

Of or pertaining to murder; characterized by, or causing, murder or bloodshed; having the purpose or quality of murder; bloody; sanguinary; as, the murderous king; murderous rapine; murderous intent; a murderous assault. "Murderous coward." Shak.  -- Mur"der*ous*ly, adv.
A little towel, or small cloth, esp. one for wiping the fingers and mouth at table.
Innumerable; countless.
A large and bright constellation on the equator, between the stars Aldebaran and Sirius. It contains a remarkable nebula visible to the naked eye. The flaming glories of Orion's belt. E. Everett.
Had or held under obligation of paying; due. There is more owing her than is paid. Shak.

To make to be at peace; to appease; to calm; to still; to quiet; to allay the agitation, excitement, or resentment of; to tranquillize; as, to pacify a man when angry; to pacify pride, appetite, or importunity. "Pray ye, pacify yourself." Shak.
imp. of Partake.
A clog or sole of wood, usually supported by an iron ring, worn to raise the feet from the wet or the mud. The patten now supports each frugal dame. Gay.

Forcibly entering, or adapted to enter, at or by a point; perforating; penetrating; keen; -- used also figuratively; as, a piercing instrument, or thrust. "Piercing eloquence." Shak.  -- Pier"cing*ly, adv.  -- Pier"cing*ness, n.
Folded; doubled over; braided; figuratively, involved; intricate; artful. Time shall unfold what plaited cunning hides. Shak.
imp. & p. p. of Plead [Colloq.] Spenser.

One who possesses; one who occupies, holds, owns, or controls; one who has actual participation or enjoyment, generally of that which is desirable; a proprietor. "Possessors of eternal glory." Law. As if he had been possessor of the whole world. Sharp.
A piece or fragment of a broken pot. Job ii. 8.
Urgent; exacting; importunate; as, a pressing necessity.  -- Press"ing*ly, adv.

One who prints; especially, one who prints books, newspapers, engravings, etc., a compositor; a typesetter; a pressman. Printer's devil, Printer's gauge. See under Devil, and Gauge.  -- Printer's ink. See Printing ink, below.

a. & n. from Quake, v. Quaking aspen (Bot.), an American species of poplar (Populus tremuloides), the leaves of which tremble in the lightest breeze. It much resembles the European aspen. See Aspen.  -- Quaking bog, a bog of forming peat so saturated with water that it shakes when trodden upon.  --
In a rush manner; with precipitation. He that doth anything rashly, must do it willingly; for he was free to deliberate or not. L'Estrange.
The state or quality of being ready; preparation; promptness; aptitude; willingness. They received the word with all readiness of mind. Acts xvii. 11.
In a regular manner; in uniform order; methodically; in due order or time.
The act of regulating, or the state of being regulated. The temper and regulation of our own minds. Macaulay.

Famous; celebrated for great achievements, for distinguished qualities, or for grandeur; eminent; as, a renowned king. "Some renowned metropolis with glistering spires." Milton. These were the renouwned of the congregation. Num. i. 61.

Having a fixed purpose; determined; resolute; -- usually placed after its noun; as, a man resolved to be rich. That makes him a resolved enemy. Jer. Taylor. I am resolved she shall not settle here. Fielding.

A brass wind instrument, like a bass trumpet, so contrived that it can be lengthened or shortened according to the tone required; -- said to be the same as the trombone. [Written also sagbut.] Moore (Encyc. of Music).
Of or pertaining to sacrifice or sacrifices; consisting in sacrifice; performing sacrifice. "Sacrificial rites." Jer. Taylor.
A precious stone, probably a carnelian, one of which was set in Aaron's breastplate. Ex. xxviii. 17.
Full of scorn or contempt; contemptuous; disdainful. Scornful of winter's frost and summer's sun. Prior. Dart not scornful glances from those eyes. Shak.
In the second place.
In a secure manner; without fear or apprehension; without danger; safely. His daring foe . . . securely him defied. Milton.
The act of stating, reciting, or presenting, orally or in paper; as, to interrupt a speaker in the statement of his case.
The quality or state of being steady. Steadiness is a point of prudence as well as of courage. L'Estrange.
In a strict manner; closely; precisely.
A fastness; a fort or fortress; fortfield place; a place of security.
In a strong manner; so as to be strong in action or in resistance; with strength; with great force; forcibly; powerfully; firmly; vehemently; as, a town strongly fortified; he objected strongly.
The quality or state of being sweet (in any sense of the adjective); gratefulness to the taste or to the smell; agreeableness.
One whose occupation is to tan hides, or convert them into leather by the use of tan.
At that place; there. Wide is the gate, and broad is the way, that leadeth to destruction, and many there be which go in thereat. Matt. vii. 13.
A tinkle, or succession of tinkles. Drowsy tinklings lull the distant folds. Gray.

A mineral occurring in rhombic prisms, generally yellowish and pellucid, also colorless, and of greenesh, bluish, or brownish shades. It sometimes occurs massive and opaque. It is a fluosilicate of alumina, and is used as a gem.
Having or exercising trust; confiding; unsuspecting; trustful.  -- Trust"ing*ly, adv.
Having two edges, or edges on both sides; as, a two-edged sword.

Not able; not having sufficient strength, means, knowledge, skill, or the like; impotent' weak; helpless; incapable; -- now usually followed by an infinitive or an adverbial phrase; as, unable for work; unable to bear fatigue. Sapless age and weak unable limbs. Shak.
Not stable; not firm, fixed, or constant; subject to change or overthrow.  -- Un*sta"ble*ness, n. Chaucer. Unstable equilibrium. See Stable equilibrium, under Stable.

Full of use, advantage, or profit; producing, or having power to produce, good; serviceable for any end or object; helpful toward advancing any purpose; beneficial; profitable; advantageous; as, vessels and instruments useful in a family; books useful for improvement; useful knowledge; useful arts.

Such as is in common use; such as occurs in ordinary practice, or in the ordinary course of events; customary; ordinary; habitual; common. Consultation with oracles was a thing very usual and frequent in their times. Hooker. We can make friends of these usual enemies. Baxter. -- U"su*al*ly, adv.  --
Highly regarded; esteemed; prized; as, a valued contributor; a valued friend. Valued policy. See under Policy.
In a vehement manner.

Of or pertaining to victory, or a victor' being a victor; bringing or causing a victory; conquering; winning; triumphant; as, a victorious general; victorious troops; a victorious day. But I shall rise victorious, and subdue My vanquisher. Milton. Now are our brows bound wind victorious wreaths. Sha
Attentive to discover and avoid danger, or to provide for safety; wakeful; watchful; circumspect; wary. "Be sober, be vigilant." 1 Pet. v. 8. Sirs, take your places, and be vigilant. Shak.
a. & n. from Wade, v. Wading bird. (Zoöl.) See Wader, 2.
See 4th Ware.

The state of being a widow; the time during which a woman is widow; also, rarely, the state of being a widower. Johnson clung to her memory during a widowhood of more than thirty years. Leslie Stephen.
The act or manner of expressing in words; style of expression; phrasing. It is believed this wording was above his known style. Milton.
p. p. of Wear. Worn land, land that has become exhausted by tillage, or which for any reason has lost its fertility.
imp. & p. p. of Wring.
Forsaken, deserted. "Your abandoned streams." Thomson.
Operating in any way.
The state or quality of being active; nimbleness; agility; vigorous action or operation; energy; active force; as, an increasing variety of human activities. "The activity of toil." Palfrey.
Taken by adoption; taken up as one's own; as, an adopted son, citizen, country, word.  -- A*dopt"ed*ly, adv.
Compensation for a loss or injury; recompense; reparation. [Now const. with sing. verb.] "An honorable amends." Addison. Yet thus far fortune maketh us amends. Shak.
Over again; another time; in a new form; afresh; as, to arm anew; to create anew. Dryden.
To give public notice, or first notice of; to make known; to publish; to proclaim. Her [Q. Elizabeth's] arrival was announced through the country by a peal of cannon from the ramparts. Gilpin.
Yearly; year by year.
In an anxious manner; with painful uncertainty; solicitously.

To excite to action from a state of rest; to stir, or put in motion or exertion; to rouse; to excite; as, to arouse one from sleep; to arouse the dormant faculties. Grasping his spear, forth issued to arouse His brother, mighty sovereign on the host. Cowper. No suspicion was aroused. Merivale.

The act or state of attending or heeding; the application of the mind to any object of sense, representation, or thought; notice; exclusive or special consideration; earnest consideration, thought, or regard; obedient or affectionate heed; the supposed power or faculty of attending. They say the ton
With the feet bare; without shoes or stockings.
A fermented liquor made from any malted grain, but commonly from barley malt, with hops or some other substance to impart a bitter flavor.
Blessed. "This patriarch blest." Milton. White these blest sounds my ravished ear assail. Trumbull.
The nuptial appartment. Matt. ix. 15.
An old spelling of Camphor.
An artificial channel filled with water and designed for navigation, or for irrigating land, etc.
In a cheerful manner, gladly.
The inner bark of the shoots of Cinnamomum Zeylanicum, a tree growing in Ceylon. It is aromatic, of a moderately pungent taste, and is one of the best cordial, carminative, and restorative spices.
In a circumspect manner; cautiously; warily.
The act or process of compiling or gathering together from various sources.
Sexual lust; morbid carnal passion. Concupiscence like a pestilence walketh in darkness. Horne.
Correspondence in form, manner, or character; resemblance; agreement; congruity; -- followed by to, with, or between.

The act of consummating, or the state of being consummated; completed; completion; perfection; termination; end (as of the world or of life). "Tis a consummation Devoutly to be wished. Shak. From its original to its consummation. Addison. Quiet consummation have, And renownShak. Consummation of marr
See Content, n.
An umbelliferous plant, the Coriandrum sativum, the fruit or seeds of which have a strong smell and a spicy taste, and in medicine are considered as stomachic and carminative.
In a correct manner; exactly; acurately; without fault or error.
One who credits, believes, or trusts. The easy creditors of novelties. Daniel.

The point of time when it is to be decided whether any affair or course of action must go on, or be modified or terminate; the decisive moment; the turning point. This hour's the very crisis of your fate. Dryden. The very times of crisis for the fate of the country. Brougham.
Same as Cumin. Ye pay tithe of mint, and cummin. Matt. xxiii. 23.

The act of one who deals; distribution of anything, as of cards to the players; method of business; traffic; intercourse; transaction; as, to have dealings with a person. Double dealing, insincere, treacherous dealing; duplicity.  -- Plain dealing, fair, sincere, honorable dealing; honest, outspoken

Tending to deceive; having power to mislead, or impress with false opinions; as, a deceptive countenance or appearance. Language altogether deceptive, and hiding the deeper reality from our eyes. Trench. Deceptive cadence (Mus.), a cadence on the subdominant, or in some foreign key, postponing the f
Free from ambiguity; unequivocal; unmistakable; unquestionable; clear; evident; as, a decided advantage. "A more decided taste for science." Prescott.
One who, or that which, devours.
An argument which presents an antagonist with two or more alternatives, but is equally conclusive against him, whichever alternative he chooses.
The principal meal of the day, eaten by most people about midday, but by many (especially in cities) at a later hour.
To annul completely; to render void or of no effect. For the Lord of hosts hath purposed, and who shall disannul Isaiah xiv. 27.

The act of dissembling; a hiding under a false appearance; concealment by feigning; false pretension; hypocrisy. Let love be without dissimulation. Rom. xii. 9. Dissimulation . . . when a man lets fall signs and arguments that he is not that he is. Bacon. Simulation is a pretense of what is not, and
An instrument, having stretched metallic wires which are beaten with two light hammers held in the hands of the performer.
In any other place; as, these trees are not to be found elsewhere.

To furnish with money or its equivalent, as a permanent fund for support; to make pecuniary provision for; to settle an income upon; especially, to furnish with dower; as, to endow a wife; to endow a public institution. Endowing hospitals and almshouses. Bp. Stillingfleet.
One who engraves; a person whose business it is to produce engraved work, especially on metal or wood.
To twist or interweave in such a manner as not to be easily separated; to make tangled, confused, and intricate; as, to entangle yarn or the hair.
Distinguished among others of the same class or kind; special; concerning a species or a single object; principal; particular; as, in an especial manner or degree.

A bringer of the glad tidings of Church and his doctrines. Specially: (a) A missionary preacher sent forth to prepare the way for a resident pastor; an itinerant missionary preacher. (b) A writer of one of the four Gospels (With the definite article); as, the four evangelists, Matthew, Mark, Luke, a

In an exact manner; precisely according to a rule, standard, or fact; accurately; strictly; correctly; nicely. "Exactly wrought." Shak. His enemies were pleased, for he had acted exactly as their interests required. Bancroft.
The act of cursing; a curse dictated by violent feelings of hatred; imprecation; utter detestation expressed. Cease, gentle, queen, these execrations. Shak.
, One who practices extortion.
A large ornamental letter used, esp. by the early printers, at the commencement of the chapters and other divisions of a book. Brande & C.
The father of one's husband or wife; -- correlative to son-in- law and daughter-in-law.

Without fault; not defective or imperfect; free from blemish; free from incorrectness, vice, or offense; perfect; as, a faultless poem. Whoever thinks a faultless piece to see, Thinks what ne'er was, nor is, nor e'er shall be. Pope.
The state of being fearful.
In a fit manner; suitably; properly; conveniently; as, a maxim fitly applied.
The act of making a fold or folds; also, a fold; a doubling; a plication. The lower foldings of the vest. Addison.
The act of forbearing or waiting; the exercise of patience. He soon shall findForbearance no acquittance ere day end. Milton.
imp. & p. p. of Forget.
Lacking, or not bearing, fruit; barren; destitute of offspring; as, a fruitless tree or shrub; a fruitless marriage. Shak.
The state of being full, or of abounding; abundance; completeness. [Written also fulness.] "In thy presence is fullness of joy." Ps. xvi. 11.

One of several small, swift, elegantly formed species of antelope, of the genus Gazella, esp. G. dorcas; -- called also algazel, corinne, korin, and kevel. The gazelles are celebrated for the luster and soft expression of their eyes. [Written also gazel.]
Given to gluttony; eating to excess; indulging the appetite; voracious; as, a gluttonous age.  -- Glut"ton*ous*ly, adv.  -- Glut"ton*ous*ness, n.
In a gracious manner; courteously; benignantly. Dryden.
A farm steward. [Obs.]
from Grind. Grinding frame, an English name for a cotton spinning machine.  -- Grinding mill. (a) A mill for grinding grain. (b) A lapidary's lathe.
See Grizzled.
A space equal to the breadth of the hand; a palm. Ex. xxxvii. 12.
A South African mullet, salted for food.
In a harsh manner; gratingly; roughly; rudely. 'T will sound harshly in her ears. Shak.
Furnished with a head (commonly as denoting intellectual faculties); -- used in composition; as, clear-headed, long-headed, thick-headed; a many-headed monster.
One who hears; an auditor.
See the Note under Her, pr.
The state of being high; elevation; loftiness.
The master or head of a family; one who occupies a house with his family. Towns in which almost every householder was an English Protestant. Macaulay. Compound householder. See Compound, a.
In what manner soever; to whatever degree or extent; however. I am glad he's come, howsoever he comes. Shak.
Farthest inward; most remote from the outward part; inmost; deepest within. Prov. xviii. 8.

One who or that which interprets, explains, or expounds; a translator; especially, a person who translates orally between two parties. We think most men's actions to be the interpreters of their thoughts. Locke.
The bone of either jaw; a maxilla or a mandible.
from Leap, to jump. Leaping house, a brothel. [Obs.] Shak.  -- Leaping pole, a pole used in some games of leaping.  -- Leaping spider (Zoöl.), a jumping spider; one of the Saltigradæ.
The time that life continues.
A horizontal member spanning an opening, and carrying the superincumbent weight by means of its strength in resisting crosswise fracture.

Of or pertaining to letters or literature; pertaining to learning or learned men; as, literary fame; a literary history; literary conversation. He has long outlived his century, the term commonly fixed as the test of literary merit. Johnson.

A comprehensive name for all of the pretended arts which claim to produce effects by the assistance of supernatural beings, or departed spirits, or by a mastery of secret forces in nature attained by a study of occult science, including enchantment, conjuration, witchcraft, sorcery, necromancy, inca
A trough or open box in which fodder is placed for horses or cattle to eat.
The slaying of a human being; destruction of men. Milton.

An implement for digging and grubbing. The head has two long steel blades, one like an adz and the other like a narrow ax or the point of a pickax. 'T is you must dig with mattock and with spade. Shak.
pl of Mouse.
Giving milk; -- now applied only to beasts. "Milch camels." Gen. xxxii. "Milch kine." Shak.

Gentle; pleasant; kind; soft; bland; clement; hence, moderate in degree or quality; -- the opposite of harsh, severe, irritating, violent, disagreeable, etc.; -- applied to persons and things; as, a mild disposition; a mild eye; a mild air; a mild medicine; a mild insanity. The rosy morn resigns her
Abounding with deep mud; full of mire; muddy; as, a miry road.
A moderate degree of wetness. Bacon.
The berry or fruit of any tree of the genus Morus; also, the tree itself. See Morus.
The quality or state of being naughty; perverseness; badness; wickedness. I know thy pride, and the naughtiness of thine heart. 1 Sam. xvii. 28.
Lately; recently. He rubbed it o'er with newly gathered mint. Dryden.
The act of nourishing, or the state of being nourished; nutrition.
Farthest from the middle or interior; farthest outward; outermost.
One who begets, or brings forth, offspring; a father or a mother. Children, obey your parents in the Lord. Eph. vi. 1.

Any large webfooted bird of the genus of Pelecanus, of which about a dozen species are known. They have an enormous bill, to the lower edge of which is attached a pouch in which captured fishes are temporarily stored.
Stocked with, or as with, people; inhabited. "The peopled air." Gray.
In a perpetual manner; constantly; continually. The Bible and Common Prayer Book in the vulgar tongue, being perpetually read in churches, have proved a kind of standard for language. Swift.
Formed with a pipe; having pipe or pipes; tubular.
See Practice.
Making a promise or promises; affording hope or assurance; as, promising person; a promising day.  -- Prom"is*ing*ly, adv.
In a prudent manner.
The state of being pure (in any sense of the adjective).

Of late origin, existence, or occurrence; lately come; not of remote date, antiquated style, or the like; not already known, familiar, worn out, trite, etc.; fresh; novel; new; modern; as, recent news. The ancients were of opinion, that a considerable portion of that country [Egypt] was recent, and
The state of being related by kindred, affinity, or other alliance. Mason.
The act of removing, or the state of being removed.
Consisting of, abounding with, or resembling, sand; full of sand; covered or sprinkled with sand; as, a sandy desert, road, or soil.
One who sells. Chaucer.
The Hebrew plural of Seraph. Cf. Cherubim.

In a sharp manner,; keenly; acutely. They are more sharply to be chastised and reformed than the rude Irish. Spenser. The soldiers were sharply assailed with wants. Hayward. You contract your eye when you would see sharply. Bacon.
The act or state of one who sojourns.
In a solemn manner; with gravity; seriously; formally. There in deaf murmurs solemnly are wise. Dryden. I do solemnly assure the reader. Swift.
The quality or state of being sound; as, the soundness of timber, of fruit, of the teeth, etc.; the soundness of reasoning or argument; soundness of faith.
Spare; saving; frugal; merciful. Bacon.  -- Spar"ing*ly, adv.  -- Spar"ing*ness, n.
imp. & p. p. of Spill. Spilled.
Having strings; as, a stringed instrument. Ps. cl. 4.
A young child or animal nursed at the breast.
Capable of suiting; fitting; accordant; proper; becoming; agreeable; adapted; as, ornaments suitable to one's station; language suitable for the subject.  -- Suit"a*ble*ness, n.  -- Suit"a*bly, adv.
Several; divers; more than one or two; various. "Sundry wines." Chaucer. "Sundry weighty reasons." Shak. With many a sound of sundry melody. Chaucer. Sundry foes the rural realm surround. Dryden.

More than is wanted or is sufficient; rendered unnecessary by superabundance; unnecessary; useless; excessive; as, a superfluous price. Shak. An authority which makes all further argument or illustration superfluous. E. Everett. Superfluous interval (Mus.), an interval that exceeds a major or perfec
See Symbolics.
Of or pertaining to Syria, or its language; as, the Syriac version of the Pentateuch.  -- n.
Brought to a proper temper; as, tempered steel; having (such) a temper; -- chiefly used in composition; as, a good-tempered or bad- tempered man; a well-tempered sword.

Of or pertaining to a tempest; involving or resembling a tempest; turbulent; violent; stormy; as, tempestuous weather; a tempestuous night; a tempestuous debate.  -- Tem*pes"tu*ous*ly, adv.  -- Tem*pes"tu*ous*ness, n. They saw the Hebrew leader, Waiting, and clutching his tempestuous beard. Longfell

One who tempts or entices; especially, Satan, or the Devil, regarded as the great enticer to evil. "Those who are bent to do wickedly will never want tempters to urge them on." Tillotson. So glozed the Tempter, and his proem tuned. Milton.
The turpentine tree.
A man who makes and leaves a will, or testament, at death.
The quality or state of being thick (in any of the senses of the adjective).
In a thorough manner; fully; entirely; completely.

a. & n. from Throw, v. Throwing engine, Throwing mill, Throwing table, or Throwing wheel (Pottery), a machine on which earthenware is first rudely shaped by the hand of the potter from a mass of clay revolving rapidly on a disk or table carried by a vertical spindle; a potter's wheel.
Small or portable articles for dress, furniture, or use; goods; luggage; things. [Colloq.]
Violation of allegiance or of faith and confidence; treasonable or perfidious conduct; perfidy; treason.

Fitted to excite fear or terror; such as may astonish or terrify by its magnitude, force, or violence; terrible; dreadful; as, a tremendous wind; a tremendous shower; a tremendous shock or fall. A tremendous mischief was a foot. Motley.

Urging; pressing; besetting; plying, with importunity; calling for immediate attention; instantly important. "The urgent hour." Shak. Some urgent cause to ordain the contrary. Hooker. The Egyptians were urgent upon the people that they might send them out of the land in haste. Ex. xii. 33.
The quality or state of being variable; variability. James i. 17.
See Villainy.

Wherewith. "Wherewithal shall we be clothed" Matt. vi. 31. Wherewithal shall a young man cleanse his way Ps. cxix. 9. [The builders of Babel], still with vain design, New Babels, had they wherewithal, would build. Milton.
Whatever person; any person who; be or she who; any one who; as, he shall be punished, whoever he may be. "Whoever envies or repines." Milton. "Whoever the king favors." Shak.
A whoremaster; a lecher; a man who frequents the society of whores.
The quality of being wide; extent from side to side; breadth; wideness; as, the width of cloth; the width of a door.
The quality or state of being willing; free choice or consent of the will; freedom from reluctance; readiness of the mind to do or forbear. Sweet is the love which comes with willingness. Dryden.

A Jewish cabalistic book attributed by tradition to Rabbi Simon ben Yochi, who lived about the end of the 1st century, a. d. Modern critics believe it to be a compilation of the 13th century. Encyc. Brit.
Father; religious superior; -- in the Syriac, Coptic, and Ethiopic churches, a title given to the bishops, and by the bishops to the patriarch.
Extreme hatred or detestation; the feeling of utter dislike.

A stone imagined by some to be of impenetrable hardness; a name given to the diamond and other substance of extreme hardness; but in modern minerology it has no technical signification. It is now a rhetorical or poetical name for the embodiment of impenetrable hardness. Opposed the rocky orb Of tenf

Being of advantage; conferring advantage; gainful; profitable; useful; beneficial; as, an advantageous position; trade is advantageous to a nation. Advabtageous comparison with any other country. Prescott. You see . . . of what use a good reputation is, and how swift and advantageous a harbinger it
On foot. We 'll walk afoot a while. Shak.
Same as Almug (and etymologically preferable). 2 Chron. ii. 8.
United; joined; leagued; akin; related. See Ally.

A variety of crystallized quartz, of a purple or bluish violet color, of different shades. It is much used as a jeweler's stone. Oriental amethyst, the violet-blue variety of transparent crystallized corundum or sapphire.
In any place. Udall.
To make quiet; to calm; to reduce to a state of peace; to still; to pacify; to dispel (anger or hatred); as, to appease the tumult of the ocean, or of the passions; to appease hunger or thirst.
A bowman, one skilled in the use of the bow and arrow.
The principal female divinity of the Phoenicians, as Baal was the principal male divinity. W. Smith.
Joined as a companion; brought into association; accompanying; combined. Associated movements (Physiol.), consensual movements which accompany voluntary efforts without our consciousness. Dunglison.
The figure of a star, thus,
To rouse from sleep or torpor; to awake; to wake. [He] is dispatched Already to awaken whom thou nam'st. Cowper. Their consciences are thoroughly awakened. Tillotson.
The hinder part, posteriors, or rump of a person or animal.
The condition of being barren; sterility; unproductiveness. A total barrenness of invention. Dryden.
An unidentified substance mentioned in the Bible (Gen. ii. 12, and Num. xi. 7), variously taken to be a gum, a precious stone, or pearls, or perhaps a kind of amber found in Arabia.
A framework for supporting a bed.

An instrument, utensil, or machine, which, by alternate expansion and contraction, or by rise and fall of the top, draws in air through a valve and expels it through a tube for various purposes, as blowing fires, ventilating mines, or filling the pipes of an organ with wind. Bellows camera, in photo

Having a disposition to do good; possessing or manifesting love to mankind, and a desire to promote their prosperity and happiness; disposed to give to good objects; kind; charitable.  -- Be*nev"o*lent*ly, adv.
Entreating urgently; imploring; as, a beseeching look.  -- Be*seech"ing*ly, adv.  -- Be*seech"ing*ness, n.
Eager to shed blood; cruel; sanguinary; murderous.  -- Blood"thirst`i*ness (n.
A female slave, or one bound to service without wages, as distinguished from a hired servant.
One who borrows. Neither a borrower nor a lender be. Shak.
A kiln, or furnace, in which bricks are baked or burnt; or a pile of green bricks, laid loose, with arches underneath to receive the wood or fuel for burning them.

Liquid in which flesh (and sometimes other substances, as barley or rice) has been boiled; thin or simple soup. I am sure by your unprejudiced discourses that you love broth better than soup. Addison.
See Buzz. [Obs.]
Affected with canker; as, a cankered mouth.
A genus of leguminous plants (herbs, shrubs, or trees) of many species, most of which have purgative qualities. The leaves of several species furnish the senna used in medicine.
A numbering of the people, and valuation of their estate, for the purpose of imposing taxes, etc.; -- usually made once in five years.
A judicial court of chancery, which in England and in the United States is distinctively a court with equity jurisdiction.
In a charitable manner.
The act of producing or bringing forth children; parturition. Milton. Addison.
The act of bringing forth a child; travail; labor. Jer. Taylor.
The ninth month of the Jewish ecclesiastical year, answering to a part of November with a part of December.
the old imp. of chide. See Chide.
Like a churl; rude; cross-grained; ungracious; surly; illiberal; niggardly. "Churlish benefits." Ld. Burleigh. Half mankind maintain a churlish strife. Cowper.
See Scimiter.
Curved; bent; as, compassing timbers.
Tending to compose or soothe.
With confidence; with strong assurance; positively.
The act or process of taking property or condemning it to be taken, as forfeited to the public use. The confiscations following a subdued rebellion. Hallam.
One who conquers. The Conqueror (Eng. Hist.). William the Norman (1027-1067) who invaded England, defeated Harold in the battle of Hastings, and was crowned king, in 1066.

The act of constraining, or the state of being constrained; that which compels to, or restrains from, action; compulsion; restraint; necessity. Long imprisonment and hard constraint. Spenser. Not by constraint, but bDryden.
On the contrary; oppositely; on the other hand. Not rendering evil for evil, or railing for railing; but contrariwise, blessing. 1 Pet. iii. 9.
The act of coupling or joining; union; conjunction. Wit, you know, is the unexpected copulation of ideas. Johnson.

From Copy, v. Copying ink. See under Ink.  -- Copying paper, thin unsized paper used for taking copies of letters, etc., in a copying press.  -- Copying press, a machine for taking by pressure, an exact copy of letters, etc., written in copying ink.
In a courteous manner.
One skilled in some trade or manual occupation; an artificer; a mechanic.
imp. & p. p. of Creep.
Delightfully; as, to feed deliciously; to be deliciously entertained.
A Roman silver coin of the value of about fourteen cents; the "penny" of the New Testament; -- so called from being worth originally ten of the pieces called as.

To mark out plainly; to signify by a visible sign; to serve as the sign or name of; to indicate; to point out; as, the hands of the clock denote the hour. The better to denote her to the doctor. Shak.

Possessed of discernment, especially in avoiding error or evil, and in the adaptation of means to ends; prudent; sagacious; judicious; not rash or heedless; cautious. It is the discreet man, not the witty, nor the learned, nor the brave, who guides the conversation, and gives measures to society. Ad

The act or process of scattering or dispersing, or the state of being scattered or separated; as, the Jews in their dispersion retained their rites and ceremonies; a great dispersion of the human family took place at the building of Babel. The days of your slaughter and of your dispersions are accom
See Distill.
Full of dole or grief; expressing or exciting sorrow; sorrowful; sad; dismal. With screwed face and doleful whine. South. Regions of sorrow, doleful shades. Milton.
The action of causing to drop or of letting drop; falling.
The state or quality of being earnest; intentness; anxiety. An honest earnestness in the young man's manner. W. Irving.
One who, or that which, eats.

Uplifted; high; lofty; also, animated; noble; as, elevated thoughts. Elevated railway, one in which the track is raised considerably above the ground, especially a city railway above the line of street travel.

One who enchants; a sorcerer or magician; also, one who delights as by an enchantment. Like ghosts from an enchanter fleeing. Shelley. Enchanter's nightshade (Bot.), a genus (Circæa) of low inconspicuous, perennial plants, found in damp, shady places.
The parts or places which surround another place, or lie in its neighborhood; suburbs; as, the environs of a city or town. Chesterfield.
, but properly a participle. With rejection or exception of; excluding; except. "Excepting your worship's presence." Shak. No one was ever yet made utterly miserable, excepting by himself. Lubbock.

To quench; to put out, as a light or fire; to stifle; to cause to die out; to put an end to; to destroy; as, to extinguish a flame, or life, or love, or hope, a pretense or a right. A light which the fierce winds have no power to extinguish. Prescott. This extinguishes my right to the reversion. Bla
A calf, lamb, kid, or other young animal fattened for slaughter; a fat animal; -- said of such animals as are used for food. He sacrificed oxen and fatlings. 2 Sam. vi. 13.
Containing faults, blemishes, or defects; imperfect; not fit for the use intended. Created once So goodly and erect, though faulty since. Milton.
Pertaining to finance. "Our financial and commercial system." Macaulay.
A piece of burning wood. L'Estrange.

A vessel with a narrow mouth, used for holding and conveying liquors. It is generally larger than a bottle, and of leather or stoneware rather than of glass. A trencher of mutton chops, and a flagon of ale. Macaulay.
To skin; to strip off the skin or surface of; as, to flay an ox; to flay the green earth. With her nails She 'll flay thy wolfish visage. Shak.
imp. of Fly.

A person belonging to or owning allegiance to a foreign country; one not native in the country or jurisdiction under consideration, or not naturalized there; an alien; a stranger. Joy is such a foreigner, So mere a stranger to my thoughts. Denham. Nor could the majesty of the English crown appear in
To warn beforehand; to give previous warning, admonition, information, or notice to; to caution in advance. We were forewarned of your coming. Shak.
Having four feet; quadruped; as, fourfooted beasts.
A sportsman who pursues wild fowl, or takes or kills for food.
To disturb with fear; to throw into a state of alarm or fright; to affright; to terrify. More frightened than hurt. Old Proverb.

To fill up; to make full or complete. [Obs.] "Fulfill her week" Gen. xxix. 27. Suffer thou that the children be fulfilled first, for it is not good to take the bread of children and give to hounds. Wyclif (Mark vii. 27).
That with which one is girded; a girdle. Instead of a stomacher, a girding of sackcloth. Is. iii. 24.

A musical instrument, of unknown character, supposed by some to have been used by the people of Gath, and thence obtained by David. It is mentioned in the title of Psalms viii., lxxxi., and lxxxiv. Dr. W. Smith.
Illustrious; honorable; noble. [Obs.] Milton.
Calvary. See the Note under Calvary.
The mother of one's father or mother.
A son's or daughter's son.
See Gree a step. [Obs.]
, adv. In a greedy manner.
In a gross manner; greatly; coarsely; without delicacy; shamefully; disgracefully.
Cautious; wary; circumspect; as, he was guarded in his expressions; framed or uttered with caution; as, his expressions were guarded.  -- Guard"edly, adv.  -- Guard"ed*ness, n.
Unsympathetic; inexorable; cruel; pitiless.  -- Hard"-heart`ed*ness, n.

The quality or state of being harsh. O, she is Ten times more gentle than her father 's crabbed, And he's composed of harshness. Shak. 'Tis not enough no harshness gives offense, The sound must seem an echo to the sense. Pope.
One who, or that which, heals.
From this time forward; henceforth.
The unit of electric induction; the induction in a circuit when the electro-motive force induced in this circuit is one volt, while the inducing current varies at the rate of one ampère a second.
A handle; especially, the handle of a sword, dagger, or the like.
A writer of history; a chronicler; an annalist. Even the historian takes great liberties with facts. Sir J. Reynolds.
In an honorable manner; in a manner showing, or consistent with, honor. The reverend abbot . . . honorably received him. Shak. Why did I not more honorably starve Dryden.

A large, strong wasp. The European species (Vespa crabro) is of a dark brown and yellow color. It is very pugnacious, and its sting is very severe. Its nest is constructed of a paperlike material, and the layers of comb are hung together by columns. The American white- faced hornet (V. maculata) is
Tending to impair or damage; injurious; mischievous; occasioning loss or injury; as, hurtful words or conduct.
Not according to, or authorized by, law; specif., contrary to, or in violation of, human law; unlawful; illicit; hence, immoral; as, an illegal act; illegal trade; illegal love. Bp. Burnet.

The act of imprisoning, or the state of being imprisoned; confinement; restraint. His sinews waxen weak and raw Through long imprisonment and hard constraint. Spenser. Every confinement of the person is an imprisonment, whether it be in a common prison, or in a private house, or even by foreibly det

Bold, with contempt or disregard; unblushingly forward; impertinent; wanting modesty; shameless; saucy. More than impudent sauciness. Shak. When we behold an angel, not to fear Is to be impudent. Dryden.
The act or business of gathering or collecting anything; especially, the gathering of the fruits of the earth; harvest. Thou shalt keep . . . the feast of ingathering. Ex. xxii. 16.
Not just; wrongful; iniquitous; culpable. [Obs.] Milton. Till the injurious Roman did extort This tribute from us, we were free. Shak.

Not limited to rules prescribed, or to usual bounds; irregular; excessive; immoderate; as, an inordinate love of the world. "Inordinate desires." Milton. "Inordinate vanity." Burke.  -- In*or"di*nate*ly, adv.  -- In*or"di*nate*ness, n.
One who goes between, or intercedes; a mediator. (a) One who interposes between parties at variance, with a view to reconcile them. (b) One who pleads in behalf of another. Milton.
Jehovah. Ps. lxviii. 4.
A Hebrew woman.
Judea; also, a district inhabited by Jews; a Jews' quarter. Chaucer. Teaching throughout all Jewry. Luke xxiii. 5.
A female relative. Shak.

A small European bird of the Plover family (Vanellus cristatus, or V. vanellus). It has long and broad wings, and is noted for its rapid, irregular fight, upwards, downwards, and in circles. Its back is coppery or greenish bronze. Its eggs are the "plover's eggs" of the London market, esteemed a del
The act, or state, of inclining; inclination; tendency; as, a leaning towards Calvinism.
A kind of precious stone. The third row a ligure, an agate, and an amethyst. Ex. xxviii. 19.
According to the primary and natural import of words; not figuratively; as, a man and his wife can not be literally one flesh.
An eager desire; a craving; a morbid appetite; an earnest wish; an aspiration. Put on my crown; I have immortal longings in me. Shak.
To lie hid; to lie in wait. Like wild beasts, lurking in loathsome den. Spenser. Let us . . . lurk privily for the innocent. Prov. i. 11.
One who lusts.
Meddlesome. Macaulay.
Having the monthly flow or discharge; menstruating.
The quality or state of being mild; as, mildness of temper; the mildness of the winter.
That minces; characterized by primness or affected nicety.

In the Middle Ages, one of an order of men who subsisted by the arts of poetry and music, and sang verses to the accompaniment of a harp or other instrument; in modern times, a poet; a bard; a singer and harper; a musician. Chaucer.
One of the posterity of Moab, the son of Lot. (Gen. xix. 37.) Also used adjectively.
For the greatest part; for the most part; chiefly; in the main.
In a necessary manner; by necessity; unavoidably; indispensably.

The art of revealing future events by means of a pretended communication with the dead; the black art; hence, magic in general; conjuration; enchantment. See Black art. This palace standeth in the air, By necromancy placèd there. Drayton.
Recently born. Shak.
The quality or state of being new; as, the newness of a system; the newness of a scene; newness of life.
See Niter.
Well known by reputation or report; eminent; celebrated; as, a noted author, or traveler.  -- Not"ed*ly, adv.  -- Not"ed*ness, n.
Not anywhere; not in any place or state; as, the book is nowhere to be found.
Frequently; often. Milton.
The lammergeir.
To throw a shadow, or shade, over; to darken; to obscure. There was a cloud that overshadowed them. Mark ix. 7.
Covered or adorned with paint; portrayed in colors. As idle as a painted ship Upon a painted ocean. Coleridge.
Any hairy caterpillar which appears in great numbers, devouring herbage, and wandering about like a palmer. The name is applied also to other voracious insects. Joel. i. 4.

Abolition of function, whether complete or partial; esp., the loss of the power of voluntary motion, with or without that of sensation, in any part of the body; palsy. See Hemiplegia, and Paraplegia. Also used figuratively. "Utter paralysis of memory." G. Eliot. Mischievous practices arising out of
One who, oe that which, perfumes.
Full of, attended with, or involving, peril; dangerous; hazardous; as, a perilous undertaking. Infamous hills, and sandy, perilous wilds. Milton.

Continuing in the same state, or without any change that destroys form or character; remaining unaltered or unremoved; abiding; durable; fixed; stable; lasting; as, a permanent impression. Eternity stands permanent and fixed. Dryden. Permanent gases (Chem. & Physics), hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and

The quality or state of being perplexed or puzzled; complication; intricacy; entanglement; distraction of mind through doubt or difficulty; embarrassment; bewilderment; doubt. By their own perplexities involved, They ravel more. Milton.
One who persecutes, or harasses. Shak.
In a perverse manner.
Stripped of hair; scant of hair; bald. [Obs.] "Pilled beard." Chaucer.
The seven daughters of Atlas and the nymph Pleione, fabled to have been made by Jupiter a constellation in the sky.
More, required to be added; positive, as distinguished from negative; -- opposed to Ant: minus.
Deprived of a poll, or of something belonging to the poll. Specifically: (a) Lopped; -- said of trees having their tops cut off.

In a possible manner; by possible means; especially, by extreme, remote, or improbable intervention, change, or exercise of power; by a chance; perhaps; as, possibly he may recover. Can we . . . possibly his love desert Milton. When possibly I can, I will return. Shak.

Having determinate limitations; exactly or sharply defined or stated; definite; exact; nice; not vague or equivocal; as, precise rules of morality. The law in this point is not precise. Bacon. For the hour precise Exacts our parting hence. Milton.

The act of promoting, advancing, or encouraging; the act of exalting in rank or honor; also, the condition of being advanced, encouraged, or exalted in honor; preferment. Milton. Promotion cometh neither from the east, nor from the west, nor from the south. Ps. lxxv. 6.
One who, or that which, purifies or cleanses; a cleanser; a refiner.
A term of reproach used by the Jews of our Savior's time, meaning "worthless."

A bow or arch exhibiting, in concentric bands, the several colors of the spectrum, and formed in the part of the hemisphere opposite to the sun by the refraction and reflection of the sun's rays in drops of falling rain.
Ravenous. [Obs.] Shak.
Newly; lately; freshly; not long since; as, advices recently received.
Throwing back light, heat, etc., as a mirror or other surface.
One who, or that which, reproves.
To take or receive again.
The act of engaging in a revel; noisy festivity; reveling. And pomp and feast and revelry. Milton.
See Rye. [Obs.] Holland. Rie grass. (Bot.) (a) A kind of wild barley (Hordeum pratense). Dr. Prior. (b) Ray grass. Dr. Prior.
The act of one who, or that which, sails; the motion of a vessel on water, impelled by wind or steam; the act of starting on a voyage.
To deposit in a place of hiding; to hide; to conceal; as, to secrete stolen goods; to secrete one's self.
The season proper for sowing. While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night, shall not cease. Gen. viii. 22.
The office or quality of a seer.
The act of selecting, or the state of being selected; choice, by preference.
Half-yearly.
The act of one who, or that which, settles; the act of establishing one's self, of colonizing, subsiding, adjusting, etc.
Separately; distinctly; apart from others; individually. There must be an auditor to check and revise each severally by itself. De Quincey.
A fold or pen for sheep; a place where sheep are collected or confined.
The part of a plow which projects downward beneath the beam, for holding the share and other working parts; -- also called standard, or post.
The quality or state of being simple; simplicity. Shak.
To place. [R.] Landor.
Having familiar knowledge united with readiness and dexterity in its application; familiarly acquainted with; expert; skillful; -- often followed by in; as, a person skilled in drawing or geometry.
The act of one who smells.
Accompanied with sorrow; sorrowful. [Obs.] Shak.
Destitute or deprived of the faculty of speech.
The quality or state of being stiff; as, the stiffness of cloth or of paste; stiffness of manner; stiffness of character. The vices of old age have the stiffness of it too. South.
One who stomachs.
from Stretch, v. Stretching course (Masonry), a course or series of stretchers. See Stretcher, 2. Britton.
Quality or state of being strict.
The quality, state, or condition of being superior; as, superiority of rank; superiority in merit.
The act of sustaining; support; maintenance; subsistence; as, the sustenance of the body; the sustenance of life.

Persons added to a jury, commonly from those in or about the courthouse, to make up any deficiency in the number of jurors regularly summoned, being like, or such as, the latter. Blount. Blackstone. (b) syntactically sing.
The act of testing or proving; trial; proof.
The act of stealing; specifically, the felonious taking and removing of personal property, with an intent to deprive the rightful owner of the same; larceny.
A wood or a collection of trees, shrubs, etc., closely set; as, a ram caught in a thicket. Gen. xxii. 13.
To ward that place; in that direction. They shall ask the way to Zion, with their faces thitherward. Jer. l. 5.
The operation, practice, or art of tilling or preparing land for seed, and keeping the ground in a proper state for the growth of crops.
Weary; fatigued; exhausted.
Having or bearing a title.

Having the property of transmitting rays of light, so that bodies can be distinctly seen through; pervious to light; diaphanous; pellucid; as, transparent glass; a transparent diamond; -- opposed to Ant: opaque. "Transparent elemental air." Milton.
Having or celebrating a triumph; victorious; triumphant.  -- Tri"umph*ing*ly, adv.
Adapted to try, or put to severe trial; severe; afflictive; as, a trying occasion or position.

Any one of numerous species of pigeons belonging to Turtur and allied genera, native of various parts of the Old World; especially, the common European species (Turtur vulgaris), which is noted for its plaintive note, affectionate disposition, and devotion to its mate.
Not reasonable; irrational; immoderate; exorbitant.  -- Un*rea"son*a*ble*ness, n.  -- Un*rea"son*a*bly, adv.

Having, or being of, no use; unserviceable; producing no good end; answering no valuable purpose; not advancing the end proposed; unprofitable; ineffectual; as, a useless garment; useless pity. Not to sit idle with so great a gift Useless, and thence ridiculous. Milton.
Acting with great force; furious; violent; impetuous; forcible; mighty; as, vehement wind; a vehement torrent; a vehement fire or heat.
Covered by, or as by, a veil; hidden. "Words used to convey a veiled meaning." Earle.
A word; a vocable. [Obs.] South.
One of the holes in floor or other plates to permit water to flow through.
At what time soever; at whatever time; whenever. Mark xiv. 7.
Into which; -- used relatively. Where is that palace whereinto foul things Sometimes intrude not Shak. The brook, whereinto he loved to look. Emerson.
To which; -- used relatively. "Whereto we have already attained." Phil. iii. 16. Whereto all bonds do tie me day by day. Shak.
Resembling a whore in character or conduct; addicted to unlawful pleasures; incontinent; lewd; unchaste.  -- Whor"ish*ly, adv.  -- Whor"ish*ness, n.
One who drinks much wine. Prov. xxiii. 20.  -- Wine"bib`bing, n.
Full of wrath; very angry; greatly incensed; ireful; passionate; as, a wrathful man.
In an abominable manner; very odiously; detestably.
Agreement; harmony; conformity. "In strict accordance with the law." Macaulay.
Regarded with wonder and delight; highly prized; as, an admired poem.
To do; in doing; as, there is nothing. "What is here ado" J. Newton.
Previously mentioned; before-mentioned. Addison.
The part of the day which follows noon, between noon and evening.
Same as Embassage. [Obs. or R.] Luke xiv. 32.
An ambush. [Obs.] 2 Chron. xiii. 13.
An umbelliferous plant (Pimpinella anisum) growing naturally in Egypt, and cultivated in Spain, Malta, etc., for its carminative and aromatic seeds.
An East Indian money of account, the sixteenth of a rupee, or about 2
A hymenopterous insect of the Linnæan genus Formica, which is now made a family of several genera; an emmet; a pismire.
One taken at random rather than by selection; anybody.
Something appended to, or accompanying, a principal or greater thing, though not necessary to it, as a portico to a house. Modesty is the appendage of sobriety. Jer. Taylor.
To belong or pertain, whether by right, nature, appointment, or custom; to relate. Things appertaining to this life. Hooker. Give it unto him to whom it appertaineth. Lev. vi. 5.
Approbation; sanction. A censor . . . without whose approval nTemple.
A fixed star of the first magnitude in the constellation Boötes.
A member of the Areopagus. Acts xvii. 34.
The highest judicial court at Athens. Its sessions were held on Mars' Hill. Hence, any high court or tribunal
Belonging to the ark. [R.] Faber.

Making, or having the disposition to make, exorbitant claims of rank or estimation; giving one's self an undue degree of importance; assuming; haughty; -- applied to persons. Arrogant Winchester, that haughty prelate. Shak.
Very wonderful; of a nature to excite astonishment; as, an astonishing event.
Etym: [See At one.] [Obs.] Down he fell atones as a stone. Chaucer.
Causing awe; appalling; awful; as, an awesome sight. Wright.

A pointed instrument for piercing small holes, as in leather or wood; used by shoemakers, saddlers, cabinetmakers, etc. The blade is differently shaped and pointed for different uses, as in the brad awl, saddler's awl, shoemaker's awl, etc.
The act of banishing, or the state of being banished. He secured himself by the banishment of his enemies. Johnson. Round the wide world in banishment we roam. Dryden.
Having a base, or having as a base; supported; as, broad-based.
An animal, probably the hippopotamus, described in Job xl. 15- 24.
To put into brisk or vigorous action; to move with life and vigor; -- usually with the reciprocal pronoun. You have so bestirred your valor. Shak. Rouse and bestir themselves ere well awake. Milton.
of Betake.
Pertaining to, or derived from, the Bible; as, biblical learning; biblical authority.
Command; order; a proclamation or notifying. "Do thou thy master's bidding." Shak.
A handbarrow or portable frame on which a corpse is placed or borne to the grave.

Mineral pitch; a black, tarry substance, burning with a bright flame; Jew's pitch. It occurs as an abundant natural product in many places, as on the shores of the Dead and Caspian Seas. It is used in cements, in the construction of pavements, etc. See Asphalt.

Burning with a blaze; as, a blazing fire; blazing torches. Sir W. Scott. Blazing star. (a) A comet. [Obs.] (b) A brilliant center of attraction. (c) (Bot.) A name given to several plants; as, to Chamælirium luteum of the Lily family; Liatris squarrosa; and Aletris farinosa, called also colicroot and
imp. & p. p. of Bleed.
Quarreling; quarrelsome; noisy. She is an irksome brawling scold. Shak.
Robbery; extortion. [Obs.]
Concisely; in few words.
Of or pertaining to a brute; as, brutal nature. "Above the rest of brutal kind." Milton.
To bring forth a calf. "Their cow calveth." Job xxi. 10.
Of or pertaining to Canaan or the Canaanites.

Attentive to examine probable effects and consequences of acts with a view to avoid danger or misfortune; prudent; circumspect; wary; watchful; as, a cautious general. Cautious feeling for another's pain. Byron. Be swift to hear; but cautious of your tongue. Watts.
According to rites and ceremonies; as, a person ceremonially unclean.

A ceasing of discontinuance, as of action, whether termporary or final; a stop; as, a cessation of the war. The temporary cessation of the papal iniquities. Motley. The day was yearly observed for a festival by cessation from labor. Sir J. Hayward. Cessation of arms (Mil.), an armistice, or truce, a
Lewdness. [Obs.] Rom. xiii. 13.
Good spirits; a state of moderate joy or gayety; alacrity.

Disposition to forgive and spare, as offenders; mildness of temper; gentleness; tenderness; mercy. Great clemency and tender zeal toward their subjects. Stowe. They had applied for the royal clemency. Macaulay.
In a close manner.
Gathered together.
In a mass, or body; in a collected state; in the aggregate; unitedly.
United closely; confederated; chemically united.
Without comfort or comforts; in want or distress; cheerless. Comfortless through turanny or might. Spenser.

To call to remembrance by a special act or observance; to celebrate with honor and solemnity; to honor, as a person or event, by some act of respect of affection, intended to preserve the remembrance of the person or event; as, to commemorate the sufferings and dying love of our Savior by the sacram
Capable of being compared; worthy of comparison. There is no blessing of life comparable to the enjoyment of a discreet and virtuous friend. Addison. -- Com"pa*ra*ble*ness, n.  -- Com"pa*ra*bly, adv.

Answering to all requirements; adeqouate; sufficient; suitable; capable; legally qualified; fit. "A competent knowledge of the world." Arrerbury. "Competent age." Grafton. "Competent statesmen." Palfrey. /"A competent witness." Bouvier.
The act of complying; a yielding; as to a desire, demand, or proposal; concession; submission. What compliances will remove dissension Swift. Ready compliance with the wishes of his people. Macaulay.

An abstract general conception; a notion; a universal. The words conception, concept, notion, should be limited to the thought of what can not be represented in the imagination; as, the thought suggested by a general term. Sir W. Hamilton.

To stoop or descend; to let one's self down; to submit; to waive the privilege of rank or dignity; to accommodate one's self to an inferior. "Condescend to men of low estate." Rom. xii. 16. Can they think me so broken, so debased With corporal servitude, that my mind ever Will condescend to such abs
A rabbit. See Cony.
By consequence; by natural or logical sequence or connection.
In a consistent manner.
Adapted to console or comfort; cheering; as, this is consoling news.

Without break, cessation, or interruption; without intervening space or time; uninterrupted; unbroken; continual; unceasing; constant; continued; protracted; extended; as, a continuous line of railroad; a continuous current of electricity. he can hear its continuous murmur. Longfellow.
A thrashing floor. Hos. ix. 1.
Answering; conformable; agreeing; suiting; as, corresponding numbers.
In a corrupt manner; by means of corruption or corrupting influences; wronfully.
The quality of being costy; expensiveness; sumptuousness.
A small house; a cot; a hut.
Same as Couch.
Same as Colter.
In a courageous manner.

Of courtlike manners; pertaining to, or exxpressive of, courtesy; characterized by courtesy; civil; obliging; well bred; polite; affable; complaisant. A patient and courteous bearing. Prescott. His behavior toward his people is grave and courteous. Fuller.
A court or inclosure attached to a house.
In a cruel manner.
Encumbrance. [Obs.] Extol not riches then, the toil of fools, The wise man's cumbrance, if not snare. Milton.
A low place between hills; a vle or valley. Where mountaines rise, umbrageous dales descend. Thomson.
With imperfect light, clearness, or knowledge; obscurely; dimly; blindly; uncertainly. What fame to future times conveys but darkly down. Dryden. so softly dark and darkly pure. Byron.
The light of day as opposed to the darkness of night; the light of the sun, as opposed to that of the moon or to artificial light.
An umpire or arbiter; a mediator. Neither is there any daysman betwixt us. Job ix. 33.
Passed away; dead; gone. The deceased, the dead person.
Suited to decorate or embellish; adorning.  -- Dec"o*ra*tive*ness, n. Decorative art, fine art which has for its end ornamentation, rather than the representation of objects or events.

A yielding of judgment or preference from respect to the wishes or opinion of another; submission in opinion; regard; respect; complaisance. Deference to the authority of thoughtful and sagacious men. Whewell. Deference is the most complicate, the most indirect, and the most elegant of all complimen
Unnatural or distorted in form; having a deformity; misshapen; disfigured; as, a deformed person; a deformed head.  -- De*form"ed*ly, adv.  -- De*form"ed*ness, n.
Cast down; afflicted; low-spirited; sad; as, a dejected look or countenance.  -- De*ject"ed*ly, adv.  -- De*ject"ed*ness, n.
Highly pleasing; delightful. Delectable both to behold and taste. Milton. -- De*lec"ta*ble*ness, n.  -- De*lec"ta*bly, adv.
Highly pleasing; affording great pleasure and satisfaction. "Delightful bowers." Spenser. "Delightful fruit." Milton.
In a desperate manner; without regard to danger or safety; recklessly; extremely; as, the troops fought desperately. She fell desperately in love with him. Addison.
Marked by despondence; given to despondence; low-spirited; as, a despondent manner; a despondent prisoner.  -- De*spond"ent*ly, adv.
The act of dictating; the act or practice of prescribing; also that which is dictated. It affords security against the dictation of laws. Paley.
In a different manner; variously.
Marked with dignity; stately; as, a dignified judge.

The act of disappointing, or the state of being disappointed; defeat or failure of expectation or hope; miscarriage of design or plan; frustration. If we hope for things of which we have not thoroughly considered the value, our disappointment will be greater our pleasure in the fruition of them. Add
Represented with wings expanded; -- applied to doves and other birds not of prey. Cussans.

The act of discomfiting, or the state of being discomfited; rout; overthrow; defeat; frustration; confusion and dejection. Every man's sword was against his fellow, and there was a very great discomfiture. 1 Sam. xiv. 20. A hope destined to end . . . in discomfiture and disgrace. Macaulay.
Disdainful. [Obs.] Revenge the jeering and disdained contempt Of this proud king. Shak.
One who disputes, or who is given to disputes; a controvertist. Where is the disputer of this world 1 Cor. i. 20.
One who dissents; one who differs in opinion, or declares his disagreement.
The staff for holding a bunch of flax, tow, or wool, from which the thread is drawn in spinning by hand. I will the distaff hold; come thou and spin. Fairfax.
Marking or expressing distinction or difference; distinguishing; characteristic; peculiar. The distinctive character and institutions of New England. Bancroft.
Mentally disordered; unsettled; mad. My distracted mind. Pope.
That distributes; dealing out. Distributing past office, an office where the mails for a large district are collected to be assorted according to their destination and forwarded.
That divines; for divining. Divining rod, a rod, commonly of witch hazel, with forked branches, used by those who pretend to discover water or metals under ground.
One who guards the entrance of a house or apartment; a porter; a janitor.
That dotes; silly; excessively fond.  -- Dot"ing*ly, adv.  -- Dot"ing*ness, n.
Sorrowful; distressful. [Obs.] " Dreary shrieks." Spenser.

Able to endure or continue in a particular condition; lasting; not perishable or changeable; not wearing out or decaying soon; enduring; as, durable cloth; durable happiness. Riches and honor are with me; yea, durable riches and righteousness. Prov. viii. 18. An interest which from its object and gr

The state or quality of lasting; continuance in time; the portion of time during which anything exists. It was proposed that the duration of Parliament should be limited. Macaulay. Soon shall have passed our own human duration. D. Webster.
In an eager manner.
Having (such or so many) ears; -- used in composition; as, long- eared-eared; sharp-eared; full-eared; ten-eared.
With effect; efficaciously.
One who embroiders.
See Immanuel. Matt. i. 23.
The act of pitching tents or forming huts, as by an army or traveling company, for temporary lodging or rest.
The condition of enjoying anything; pleasure or satisfaction, as in the possession or occupancy of anything; possession and use; as, the enjoyment of an estate.
The state or quality of exceeding a measure or rule, or of being immoderate, monstrous, or outrageous. The enormity of his learned acquisitions. De Quincey.
To catch in a snare. See Insnare.
The act of equipping, or the state of being equipped, as for a voyage or expedition. Burke. The equipment of the fleet was hastened by De Witt. Hume.
To shun; to avoid, as something wrong, or from a feeling of distaste; to keep one's self clear of. They must not only eschew evil, but do good. Bp. Beveridge.
In an everlasting manner.
Having power to examine; appointed to examine; as, an examining committee.

The act of exerting, or putting into motion or action; the active exercise of any power or faculty; an effort, esp. a laborious or perceptible effort; as, an exertion of strength or power; an exertion of the limbs or of the mind; it is an exertion for him to move, to-day.
Producing exhaustion; as, exhausting labors.  -- Ex*haust"ing, adv.
Heaving (such or so many) eyes; -- used in composition; as sharp-eyed; dull-eyed; sad-eyed; ox-eyed Juno; myriad-eyed.
Service performed only under inspection, or the eye of an employer. Not with eyeservice, as menpleasers. Col. iii. 22.
In a fearful manner.
The quality or condition of being feeble; debility; infirmity. That shakes for age and feebleness. Shak.
In a firm manner.
from Flog, v. t. Flogging chisel (Mach.), a large cold chisel, used in chipping castings.  -- Flogging hammer, a small sledge hammer used for striking a flogging chisel.
To have previous knowledge of; to know beforehand. Who would the miseries of man foreknow Dryden.

, or (strictly) p. p. Provided; in case that; on condition that. [Obs.] One manner of meat is most sure to every complexion, foreseen that it be alway most commonly in conformity of qualities, with the person that eateth. Sir T. Elyot.
An unmarried person, male or female, who has criminal intercourse with the other sex; one guilty of fornication.
Ready or about to appear; making appearance.

[fragrans. -antis, p.pr. of fragrare to emit a smell of fragrance: cf. OF. fragrant. Affecting the olfactory nerves agreeably; sweet of smell; odorous; having or emitting an agreeable perfume. Fragrant the fertile earth After soft showers. Milton.
In a frank manner; freely. Very frankly he confessed his treasons. Shak.
Marked with freckles; spotted. "The freckled trout." Dryden. The freckled cowslip, burnet, and green clover. Shak.
One who enjoys liberty, or who is not subject to the will of another; one not a slave or vassal.
Affected with frenzy; frantic; maddened.  -- Fren"zied-ly, adv. The people frenzied by centuries of oppression. Buckle. Up starting with a frenzied look. Sir W Scott.
imp. & p. p. of Fry.

The act of furthering or helping forward; promotion; advancement; progress. I know that I shall abide and continue with you all for your furthersnce and joy of faith. Phil. i. 25. Built of furtherance and pursuing, Not of spent deeds, but of doing. Emerson.
One who gazes.
A person or thing gazed at with scorn or abhorrence; an object of curiosity or contempt. Bp. Hall.
In a gentle manner. My mistress gently chides the fault I made. Dryden.
State of being gloomy. Addison.
Imperfectly illuminated; dismal through obscurity or darkness; dusky; dim; clouded; as, the cavern was gloomy. "Though hid in gloomiest shade." Milton.
In a gradual manner.
The state of being grateful; warm and friendly feeling toward a benefactor; kindness awakened by a favor received; thankfulness. The debt immense of endless gratitude. Milton.
The sediment of melted tallow. It is made into cakes for dogs' food. In Scotland it is called cracklings. [Written also graves.]
Somewhat green; having a tinge of green; as, a greenish yellow.  -- Green"ish*ness, n.
See Gray (the correct orthography).
A slender, graceful breed of dogs, remarkable for keen sight and swiftness. It is one of the oldest varieties known, and is figured on the Egyptian monuments. [Written also grayhound.]
Free from guile; artless.  -- Guile"less*ly, adv. Guile"less*ness, n.

An exclamation denoting surprise, joy, or grief. Both as uttered and as written, it expresses a great variety of emotions, determined by the tone or the context. When repeated, ha, ha, it is an expression of laughter, satisfaction, or triumph, sometimes of derisive laughter; or sometimes it is equiv
A storm accompanied with hail; a shower of hail.
Work done by the hands; hence, any work done personally. The firmament showeth his handiwork. Ps. xix. 1.
A touching, controlling, managing, using, etc., with the hand or hands, or as with the hands. See Handle, v. t. The heavens and your fair handling Have made you master of the field this day. Spenser.
The cast or form of writing peculiar to each hand or person; chirography.
See Handiwork.
A man engaged in harvesting. Shak.

A covering or ornament for the head; a headtire. Among birds the males very often appear in a most beautiful headdress, whether it be a crest, a comb, a tuft of feathers, or a natural little plume. Addison.

The quarters or place of residence of any chief officer, as the general in command of an army, or the head of a police force; the place from which orders or instructions are issued; hence, the center of authority or order. The brain, which is the headquarters, or office, of intelligence. Collier.
The principal stone in a foundation; the chief or corner stone. Ps. cxviii. 22.
From the heart; with all the heart; with sincerity. I heartily forgive them. Shak.
Any discharge of blood from the blood vessels.

A thorny tree or shrub of the genus Lawsonia (L. alba). The fragrant white blossoms are used by the Buddhists in religious ceremonies. The powdered leaves furnish a red coloring matter used in the East to stain the hails and fingers, the manes of horses, etc.
Of this; concerning this; from this; hence. Hereof comes it that Prince Harry is valiant. Shak.
Unto this; up to this time; hereto.
One who hews.
In the manner of, or in accordance with, history.
A religious festival.
Native; indigenous; not foreign. Donne. Pope.
In a manner to excite horror; dreadfully; terribly.
The quality of being humble; humility; meekness.
Virtually involved or included; involved in substance; inferential; tacitly conceded; -- the correlative of express, or expressed. See Imply.
Angered; enraged.

To move to action; to stir up; to rouse; to spur or urge on. Anthiochus, when he incited Prusias to join in war, set before him the greatness of the Romans. Bacon. No blown ambition doth our arms incite. Shak.
Not capable of being contained within limits. An infinite and incomprehensible substance. Hooker.
The state or quality of being insensible; want of sensibility; torpor; unconsciousness; as, the insensibility produced by a fall, or by opiates.
In an inseparable manner or condition; so as not to be separable. Bacon. And cleaves through life inseparably close. Cowper.

To set within something; to put or thrust in; to introduce; to cause to enter, or be included, or contained; as, to insert a scion in a stock; to insert a letter, word, or passage in a composition; to insert an advertisement in a newspaper. These words were very weakly inserted where they will be so
See Instructor.
One who instructs; one who imparts knowledge to another; a teacher.

The quality or condition of being instrumental; that which is instrumental; anything used as a means; medium; agency. The instrumentality of faith in justification. Bp. Burnet. The discovery of gunpowder developed the science of attack and defense in a new instrumentality. J. H. Newman.
Strained; tightly drawn; kept on the stretch; strict; very close or earnest; as, intense study or application; intense thought.
Engaging the attention; exciting, or adapted to excite, interest, curiosity, or emotion; as, an interesting story; interesting news. Cowper.

To become connected by marriage between their members; to give and take mutually in marriage; -- said of families, ranks, castes, etc. About the middle of the fourth century from the building of Rome, it was declared lawful for nobles and plebeians to intermarry. Swift.
In an intimate manner.
Destitute of religion; not controlled by religious motives or principles; ungodly. Cf. Impiou. Shame and reproach are generally the portion of the impious and irreligious. South.
See Hyacinth. Tennyson.
The art or trade of a jeweler. Cotgrave.

To work and press into a mass, usually with the hands; esp., to work, as by repeated pressure with the knuckles, into a well mixed mass, as the materials of bread, cake, etc.; as, to knead dough. The kneading, the making of the cake, the heating of the oven, and the baking. Shak.
The act of loading.
Lamentation. Lamentings heard i' the air. Shak.
Having an estate in land. The House of Commons must consist, for the most part, of landed men. Addison.
An action at law; a suit in equity or admiralty; any legal proceeding before a court for the enforcement of a claim.
Made of leather; consisting of. leather; as, a leathern purse. "A leathern girdle about his loins." Matt. iii. 4.
Bearing, or having, a leaf or leaves; having folds; -- used in combination; as, a four-leaved clover; a two-leaved gate; long- leaved.
In a legal manner.
One who lends. The borrower is servant to the lender. Prov. xxii. 7.
Loins. [Obs.] Wyclif.

Containing or involving a libel; defamatory; containing that which exposes some person to public hatred, contempt, or ridicule; as, a libelous pamphlet. [Written also libellous.] -- Li"bel*ous*ly, adv.
A name sometimes applied to the process of annealing metals.
A female lion.
Like a lion; brave as a lion.
The state or quality of being little; as, littleness of size, thought, duration, power, etc.
A rocking or balanced stone. Gwill.
Secluded from society; not frequented by human beings; solitary. Like one that on a lonesome road Doth walk in fear and dread. Coleridge .
The calling sound made by cows and other bovine animals.

Moderately warm; neither cold nor hot; tepid; not ardent; not zealous; cool; indifferent. " Lukewarm blood." Spenser. " Lukewarm patriots." Addison. An obedience so lukewarm and languishing that it merits not the name of passion. Dryden. -- Luce"warm`ly, adv.  -- Luce"warm`ness, n.
One who kills a human being; one who commits manslaughter.
The state of being a member.
of Methinks.
Being in the middle, or nearest the middle; midmost.
To remove from one country or region to another, with a view to residence; to change one's place of residence; to remove; as, the Moors who migrated from Africa into Spain; to migrate to the West.
A certain measure of distance, being equivalent in England and the United States to 320 poles or rods, or 5,280 feet.

In the widest sense, the whole military force of a nation, including both those engaged in military service as a business, and those competent and available for such service; specifically, the body of citizens enrolled for military instruction and discipline, but not subject to be called into actual
Seated or serving on horseback or similarly; as, mounted police; mounted infantry.
One who, or that which, mows; a mowing machine; as, a lawn mower.
Fortification; stronghold. [Obs.] His place of defense shall be the munitions of rocks. Is. xxxiii. 16.

Apt to neglect; customarily neglectful; characterized by negligence; careless; heedless; culpably careless; showing lack of attention; as, disposed in negligent order. "Be thou negligent of fame." Swift. He that thinks he can afford to be negligent is not far from being poor. Rambler.
Living or being near; adjacent; as, the neighboring nations or countries.
No; not. See No, a. Chaucer.
Promoting growth; nutritious,
Giving particular attention; habitually attentive to what passes; as, an observing person; an observing mind.  -- Ob*serv"ing*ly, adv.

A fixedness in will, opinion, or resolution that can not be shaken at all, or only with great difficulty; firm and usually unreasonable adherence to an opinion, purpose, or system; unyielding disposition; stubborness; pertinacity; persistency; contumacy. You do not well in obstinacy To cavil in the
Hateful; deserving or receiving hatred; as, an odious name, system, vice. "All wickedness will be most odious." Sprat. He rendered himself odious to the Parliament. Clarendon.
That which is scoured off; hence, refuse; rejected matter; that which is vile or despised. Lam. iii. 45.
Covered or treated with oil; dressed with, or soaked in, oil. Oiled silk, silk rendered waterproof by saturation with boiled oil.

A large bird of the genus Struthio, of which Struthio camelus of Africa is the best known species. It has long and very strong legs, adapted for rapid running; only two toes; a long neck, nearly bare of feathers; and short wings incapable of flight. The adult male is about eight feet high.

Of the nature of an outrage; exceeding the limits of right, reason, or decency; involving or doing an outrage; furious; violent; atrocious. "Outrageous weeping." Chaucer. "The most outrageous villainies." Sir P. Sidney. "The vile, outrageous crimes." Shak. "Outrageous panegyric." Dryden.
A superficial covering; a coating.

The quality or condition of being pale; want of freshness or ruddiness; a sickly whiteness; lack of color or luster; wanness. The blood the virgin's cheek forsook; A livid paleness spreads o'er all her look. Pope.
The skin of a lamb, sheep, goat, young calf, or other animal, prepared for writing on. See Vellum. But here's a parchment with the seal of Cæsar. Shak.

Absence of resources; want; privation; indigence; extreme poverty; destitution. "A penury of military forces." Bacon. They were exposed to hardship and penury. Sprat. It arises in neither from penury of thought. Landor.

Through; by means of; through the agency of; by; for; for each; as, per annum; per capita, by heads, or according to individuals; per curiam, by the court; per se, by itself, of itself. Per is also sometimes used with English words. Per annum, by the year; in each successive year; annually.  -- Per
By chance; perhaps; peradventure.
See Phoebe.
Languishing; drooping; wasting away, as with longing.
The act of advocating, defending, or supporting, a cause by arguments.

The mutual pleas and replies of the plaintiff and defendant, or written statements of the parties in support of their claims, proceeding from the declaration of the plaintiff, until issue is joined, and the question made to rest on some single point. Blackstone.
The state or quality of being pleasant.
See Plow.
Adapted or tending to pollute; causing defilement or pollution.  -- Pol*lut"ing*ly, adv.
